pr:section.pr:title: Decision Layer
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Structured Architecture Decision Records with status lifecycle, alternatives, and supersession chains.
pr:section.pr:p: Prose captures the narrative context of a decision: what problem existed, what options were considered, what was chosen. But prose alone cannot express the structured metadata that tooling needs: a machine-queryable status (proposed, accepted, deprecated, superseded), a list of rejected alternatives with individual rationales, or a typed supersession chain linking a decision to the one it replaced.

The decision layer separates this structured metadata from the narrative. Each dec:decision element references a prose section (via ref) that holds the full context, while carrying its own id to participate in the spec graph as an addressable node. This means the same prose section can be read as a standalone document by a developer, while tooling can query all accepted decisions, find supersession chains, or enumerate rejected alternatives without parsing natural language.

The status lifecycle follows a linear progression. A decision starts as proposed, becomes accepted when adopted, may be deprecated when a replacement is under consideration, and becomes superseded when a new decision replaces it. The dec:supersedes element records the replacement chain: it is an extension relation type (see ) that participates in the connectivity graph, with acyclicity enforced to prevent circular supersession.

Each decision carries zero or more dec:alternative children, each with a title and rationale. Alternatives document rejected options: not just what was chosen, but what was considered and why it lost. This is the distinguishing feature over plain prose: a structured record that tooling can enumerate, compare, and report on.

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding how the decision layer captures ADR metadata separately from prose, enabling machine-queryable status tracking, alternative enumeration, and supersession chains.

pr:section.pr:title: Use XML as the Specification Format
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Specifications must be comprehensible and authorable by both LLMs and programmatic tools.
pr:section.pr:p: The format must support schema-validated structure that tools can reliably parse, query (XPath), and transform (XSLT), while remaining readable enough for LLMs to understand and generate without special training. XML satisfies both constraints: it has mature schema languages (XSD), query languages (XPath), and transformation languages (XSLT), while its tag-based syntax is well-represented in LLM training data.

The choice of XML also enables namespace-based extensibility: new semantic dimensions can be added without modifying existing schemas, because each layer occupies its own namespace. This is the foundation for the open-world extensibility model.

dec:alternative.dec:title: JSON with JSON Schema
dec:alternative.dec:rationale: No mixed content for prose, no namespace extensibility, poor readability for long-form technical content.

dec:alternative.dec:title: YAML
dec:alternative.dec:rationale: No formal schema comparable to XSD, significant whitespace creates fragile documents, no native cross-document validation.

dec:alternative.dec:title: Markdown with frontmatter
dec:alternative.dec:rationale: No structural validation, no typed cross-references. LLMs can parse it but tooling cannot enforce constraints.

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding why XML was chosen as the specification format, given the dual requirement of LLM comprehension and programmatic tool support.

pr:section.pr:title: Layered Semantics via Open-World Schema Extensibility
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Specifications gain precision incrementally through new semantic layers, each with its own XSD namespace.
pr:section.pr:p: Each layer adds a new semantic dimension (prose, terminology, organization, relation, artifact, LLM, decision) via its own XSD namespace and schema. New layers can be added without modifying existing schemas because spec.xsd uses xs:any namespace="##other" processContents="strict". This open-world extensibility means the format evolves by accretion, not mutation.

The layered model separates concerns: prose writers need not understand artifact mapping schemas, and tool authors can consume only the layers relevant to their task. Each layer's schema is independently versioned and validated.

dec:alternative.dec:title: Monolithic schema
dec:alternative.dec:rationale: Adding a semantic dimension requires modifying the central schema, coupling all layers. Every change risks breaking existing content.

dec:alternative.dec:title: Plugin system with runtime registration
dec:alternative.dec:rationale: Introduces a runtime dependency and configuration step. XSD namespaces provide the same extensibility declaratively with schema-level validation.

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding the open-world extensibility model that allows new semantic layers to be added without modifying existing schemas.

pr:section.pr:title: Topic-Organized Files with Single Root Element
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Files mix content from multiple layers for the same topic, all sharing spec:clayers as root element.
pr:section.pr:p: A file about "authorization" mixes prose, organization typing, relations, artifacts, and LLM descriptions for the same topic. This enables atomic reviews and co-located cross-layer content. Every file uses spec:clayers as its root element, distinguished only by child namespaces. This simplifies tooling (one root element to detect) and enables the index back-reference mechanism (spec:index attribute on root).

The single-root model means any tool that can parse a clayers file can process any file in the spec, regardless of which layers it contains. Layer detection is namespace-based, not root-element-based.

dec:alternative.dec:title: One file per layer
dec:alternative.dec:rationale: Editing a topic requires touching N files, violates locality, blocks atomic reviews.

dec:alternative.dec:title: Per-layer root elements
dec:alternative.dec:rationale: Tooling must know all layer root element names, breaks open-world extensibility, no shared back-reference mechanism.

dec:alternative.dec:title: One file per node
dec:alternative.dec:rationale: Fragment explosion, no file-level coherence, impractical for cross-layer co-location.

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding why files are organized by topic rather than by layer, and why all files share the same root element.

pr:section.pr:title: XSD 1.1 for Schema Validation
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: XSD 1.1 provides xs:assert for cross-attribute constraints, xs:any with processContents="strict", and xs:keyref for cross-layer referential integrity.
pr:section.pr:p: The clayers format requires conditional attribute constraints (e.g., to-revision required when to-spec is present), open-world extensibility via xs:any with processContents="strict", and cross-layer referential integrity via xs:unique and xs:keyref. Only XSD 1.1 provides all three capabilities in a single schema language.
The xs:assert facility enables constraints that would otherwise require Schematron or custom validation code, keeping all structural validation within the schema system.

dec:alternative.dec:title: XSD 1.0
dec:alternative.dec:rationale: No xs:assert for conditional attribute requirements, limited xs:any control.

dec:alternative.dec:title: RelaxNG
dec:alternative.dec:rationale: No keyref equivalent for cross-document reference validation, weaker Python tooling ecosystem.

dec:alternative.dec:title: Schematron only
dec:alternative.dec:rationale: No structural validation, only constraint rules. Current Python Schematron libraries lack document() for cross-file validation.

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding why XSD 1.1 was chosen over other schema validation approaches, and which specific features drive the requirement.

pr:section.pr:title: Migrate from Bare @id to xml:id
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: All Clayers content will eventually use W3C xml:id instead of bare @id for element addressability.
pr:section.pr:p: Clayers currently uses bare @id attributes for element addressability. This works for Clayers-native content but creates a conflict when integrating external standards like XMI/UML, which use xml:id (the W3C standard, namespace http://www.w3.org/XML/1998/namespace). Bare @id is a local, unnamespaced convention; xml:id is a universally recognized W3C standard that any XML processor understands.

During the transition period, the validator, connectivity analyzer, and coverage tools recognize both @id and xml:id. Both participate in uniqueness checking, cross-layer reference resolution, and graph construction. An element may carry either or both (though carrying both with different values on the same element is discouraged).

The migration path is: new content (especially XMI/UML model files) uses xml:id from the start. Existing content continues to use bare @id until a bulk migration is performed. Once all content uses xml:id, bare @id support can be removed from the tooling.

dec:alternative.dec:title: Keep bare @id permanently
dec:alternative.dec:rationale: Works for Clayers-native content but conflicts with XMI/UML standards. External tools and specifications use xml:id; maintaining a parallel convention creates friction and prevents reuse of standard XML tooling for ID resolution.

dec:alternative.dec:title: Immediate bulk migration
dec:alternative.dec:rationale: High risk of breaking all existing artifact mappings, cross-references, and external tooling in a single step. Gradual migration with dual support is safer.

dec:alternative.dec:title: Use xmi:id for XMI content only
dec:alternative.dec:rationale: xmi:id is XMI-specific, not a universal standard. xml:id works across all XML vocabularies and is recognized by standard XML processors without namespace-specific configuration.

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding the migration strategy from bare @id to W3C xml:id, the transition period with dual support, and why xml:id is the target convention.

spec:clayers.art:exempt: Architecture Decision Record prose. Design rationale, not a code-mapped node.
Decision metadata for an ADR. Structured record, not a code-mapped node.

rel:relation.rel:note: The decision layer adds ADR metadata as a semantic dimension within the layered architecture.
The choice of XML is a foundational decision for the layered architecture.
Open-world schema extensibility is the mechanism that enables the layered architecture.
Topic-organized files with a single root element are how the layered architecture manifests in the file system.
XSD 1.1 provides the schema features that make the layered architecture validatable.
The xml:id migration enables standard identification across all layers, including external standards like XMI/UML.
Decision metadata for the xml:id migration ADR.
Layered schemas depend on XML's namespace mechanism.
Topic-organized files depend on layered schemas to mix content from multiple layers in a single file.
XSD 1.1 is an XML schema language, so depends on the XML format decision.

trm:term.trm:name: DecisionType
trm:term.trm:definition: The XSD complexType that defines the structure of decision elements in the decision layer.

trm:term.trm:name: SupersedesType
trm:term.trm:definition: The XSD complexType that defines the structure of supersession chains in the decision layer.

rel:relation.rel:note: Activates the supersedes extension relation type defined in decision.xsd, enabling supersession chains in the connectivity graph.
The DecisionType complexType (id="type-decision") is the schema definition for decision elements in the decision layer.
The SupersedesType complexType (id="type-supersedes") is the schema definition for supersession chains in the decision layer.
The decision layer's supersedes element is an extension relation type, depending on the extensible relation type mechanism for activation and graph integration.
Decision metadata for the XML format ADR.
Decision metadata for the layered schemas ADR.
Decision metadata for the topic files ADR.
Decision metadata for the XSD 1.1 ADR.

spec:clayers.llm:node: Describes the decision layer: structured ADR metadata separate from prose. Each dec:decision references a prose section (ref) and has its own id. Status lifecycle: proposed, accepted, deprecated, superseded. Alternatives record rejected options with title and rationale. The dec:supersedes element is an extension relation type for supersession chains, activated via a refines edge to type-relation-kind. The four ADRs in this file (XML format, layered schemas, topic files, XSD 1.1) are the first instances of the decision layer applied to the format's own design.

ADR: Use XML as the specification format. XML satisfies the dual requirement of LLM comprehension (tag-based syntax well-represented in training data) and programmatic tool support (XSD validation, XPath querying, XSLT transformation). Namespace-based extensibility enables the open-world layer model. Alternatives rejected: JSON (no mixed content, no namespaces), YAML (no comparable schema language), Markdown (no structural validation).

ADR: Layered semantics via open-world schema extensibility. Each layer adds a semantic dimension via its own XSD namespace. New layers are added without modifying existing schemas because spec.xsd uses xs:any namespace="##other" processContents="strict". This means the format evolves by accretion, not mutation. Alternatives rejected: monolithic schema (coupling), plugin system (runtime dependency).

ADR: Topic-organized files with single root element. Files mix content from multiple layers for the same topic, enabling atomic reviews. All files use spec:clayers as root, distinguished by child namespaces. One root simplifies tooling and enables the index back-reference mechanism. Alternatives rejected: one file per layer (violates locality), per-layer roots (breaks extensibility), one file per node (fragment explosion).

ADR: XSD 1.1 for schema validation. Required for xs:assert (conditional attribute constraints like to-revision required when to-spec present), xs:any with processContents="strict" (open-world extensibility), and xs:unique/xs:keyref (cross-layer referential integrity). Alternatives rejected: XSD 1.0 (no xs:assert), RelaxNG (no keyref), Schematron only (no structural validation).
Decision metadata for the XML format ADR. Status: accepted. Three rejected alternatives: JSON with JSON Schema, YAML, Markdown with frontmatter.
Decision metadata for the layered schemas ADR. Status: accepted. Two rejected alternatives: monolithic schema, plugin system with runtime registration.
Decision metadata for the topic files ADR. Status: accepted. Three rejected alternatives: one file per layer, per-layer root elements, one file per node.
Decision metadata for the XSD 1.1 ADR. Status: accepted. Three rejected alternatives: XSD 1.0, RelaxNG, Schematron only.

ADR: Migrate from bare @id to W3C xml:id. Current bare @id convention conflicts with XMI/UML standards that use xml:id. Transition strategy: dual support (both @id and xml:id recognized by validator, connectivity, coverage), new content uses xml:id, eventual bulk migration of existing content. Alternatives rejected: keep bare @id permanently (friction with standards), immediate bulk migration (high breakage risk), xmi:id only (XMI-specific, not universal).
Decision metadata for the xml:id migration ADR. Status: accepted. Three rejected alternatives: keep bare @id permanently, immediate bulk migration, use xmi:id for XMI content only.
spec:clayers.llm:schema: The decision schema captures Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) as structured metadata. Each <dec:decision> references a prose section (via ref) for narrative context and carries its own id for cross-referencing. Status lifecycle: proposed, accepted, deprecated, superseded. Structured alternatives record rejected options with title and rationale. The <dec:supersedes> element is an extension relation type for supersession chains, activated by a refines edge to type-relation-kind.

An Architecture Decision Record. The ref attribute points to the prose section with the narrative. The id attribute makes it an addressable node. Contains a required status element, optional date, and zero or more alternative elements recording rejected options.
A rejected alternative for a decision. Contains a title (naming the option) and a rationale (explaining why it was rejected). Optional id attribute for individual addressability.

An extension relation type for decision supersession chains. The from attribute names the new decision, the to attribute names the old one. Acyclic: a decision cannot transitively supersede itself. Must be activated by a refines edge from type-supersedes to type-relation-kind in spec content.
spec:clayers.art:exempt: Conceptual documentation describing the decision layer's purpose and design. No code implements this concept.
Architecture Decision Record prose. Design rationale, not a code-mapped node.
Architecture Decision Record prose. Design rationale, not a code-mapped node.
Architecture Decision Record prose. Design rationale, not a code-mapped node.
Architecture Decision Record prose. Design rationale, not a code-mapped node.
Decision metadata for an ADR. Structured record, not a code-mapped node.
Decision metadata for an ADR. Structured record, not a code-mapped node.
Decision metadata for an ADR. Structured record, not a code-mapped node.
Decision metadata for an ADR. Structured record, not a code-mapped node.

art:mapping.art:note: DecisionType complexType: status, date, alternative, rationale children.
SupersedesType complexType: ref attribute linking to superseded decision.
Schema header, decision and alternative elements, DecisionType, AlternativeType, StatusKind.
SupersedesType, extension relation type annotation, and remaining type definitions.
ADR section in decisions.xml documenting the design decision.
ADR section in decisions.xml documenting the design decision.
ADR section in decisions.xml documenting the design decision.
ADR section in decisions.xml documenting the design decision.
